concepts.title: Physical Properties of Alcohol
concepts.conceptBody:
Physical Properties:

  • The polar nature of the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond (due to the electronegativity different of the atoms) results in the formation of hydrogen bonds with other alcohols molecules or other H-bonding systems (e.g. water). The implications of this are:
  • High melting and boiling points compared to analogous alkanes
  • High solubility in aqueous media

concepts.title: Structure of Alcohol
concepts.conceptBody:
Structure:

  • The alcohol functional group consists of an $\text{O}$ atom bonded to a $\text{C}$ atom and a $\text{H}$ atom via $\sigma $ bonds.
  • Both the $\text{C}-\text{O}$ and the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bonds are polar due to the high electronegativity of the $\text{O}$ atom.
  • Alcohols are bent shaped molecules. The central ‘$O$’ atom is also $s{{p}^{3}}$ hybridised and the $\text{C}-\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond angle is 105

concepts.title: Reactivity of Alcohol
concepts.conceptBody:
Reactivity:

  • The alcohol $\text{O}$ atom are a region of high electron, density due to the lone pairs.
  • Alcohol oxygen atoms are Lewis bases.
  • So alcohols can react as either bases or nucleophiles at the oxygen.
  • There is low electron density on $\text{H}$ atom of the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ group alcohol, i.e. ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$character.
  • So alcohols are acidic (pKa 16).
  • The -OH group is a poor leaving group and needs to be converted to a better leaving group before substitution can occur.

concepts.title: Solubility of Alcohol
concepts.conceptBody:
Solubility of alcohols:

The first three members are completely miscible with water. The solubility rapidly decreases with increase in molecular mass. The higher members are almost insoluble in water but are soluble in organic solvents like benzene, ether etc. The solubility of lower alcohols is due to the existence of hydrogen bonds between water and polar -OH group of alcohol molecules. Phenols too are sparingly soluble in water. The -OH group in alcohols contains a hydrogen bonded to an electronegative oxygen atom. Thus they form hydrogen bonds with water molecules.

The solubility of alcohols in water decreases with increase in molecular mass because greater carbon content makes alcohol less hydrophilic. In addition, among the isomeric alcohols, the solubility increases with branching of chain. As the surface area of the non-polar part in the molecule decreases, the solubility increases.

concepts.title: Acidity of Alcohol
concepts.conceptBody:
Acidity:

  • Due to the electronegativity of the $O$ atoms, alcohols are slightly acidic
  • The anion derived by the deprotonation of an alcohol is the alkoxide .
  • Alkoxides are important bases in organic chemistry.
  • Alcohols react with $\text{Na}$(or $\text{K}$) like water to give the alkoxide:

$2\text{R}-\text{O}-\text{H}+2\text{Na}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{NaOR}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

$2\text{H}-\text{O}-\text{H}+2\text{Na}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{NaOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

NOTE: Alcohols containing more than one $-\text{OH}$ group linked to the same carbon atom are highly unstable and lose water to form more stable aldehydes or ketones.

EXCEPTION: Few exception to the above point can be noted.

In this compound, owing to the presence of hydrogen bonding, alcoholic content is found to be considerably higher.

concepts.title: Classification of Alcohols
concepts.conceptBody:
Classification of alcohols:

(1) Monohydric alcohols$\to $ only one $-\text{OH}$ group is present.

For example

$\underset{\text{Ethanol}}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{OH}}}\,$ 

(2) Dihydric alcohol: two \[-OH\] groups are present in same molecule.

(3) Trihydric alcohols: three \[-OH\]groups present.

concepts.title: Preparation of Alcohols
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparation Of R-OH

A summary of all the methods of preparation is given below.

concepts.title: From Alkyl Halides
concepts.conceptBody:
From alkyl Halides

Alkyl halides when boiled with aqueous solution of $\text{KOH}$ or moist silver oxide furnish alcohols.

${{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{Br}+\text{KOH}(\text{aq})\to {{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{OH}+\text{KBr}$

Primary alkyl halides give good yield of alcohols. However, tertiary alkyl halides mainly undergo dehydrohalogenation resulting in formation of alkenes.

concepts.title: From Hydroboration of Alkene
concepts.conceptBody:
Hydroboration / oxidation of Alkenes

Alkenes react with diborane to form trialkyl boranes, which upon treatment with alkaline ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ give alcohols via anti-Markowni koff’s addition of water. Also, it is a ‘syn’ reaction.

For example:

concepts.title: By Oxymercuration - Dimercuration
concepts.conceptBody:
By oxymercuration-demercuration

Alkenes can be converted into alcohols by oxymercuration-demercuration reaction. In this reaction, addition of water takes place according to Markownikoff’s rule.

Mechanism:

concepts.title: From Aldehyde and Ketone by Using Grignard Reagent
concepts.conceptBody:
From Aldehydes And Ketones

 By using Grignard Reagents

When a solution of an alkyl halide in dry ethyl ether, ${{\left( {{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}} \right)}_{2}}\text{O}$, is allowed to stand over turnings of metallic magnesium, a vigorous reaction takes place. The resulting solution is known as Grignard reagent. It is one of the most useful and versatile reagents known.

$\underset{\text{ Alkyl halide }}{\mathop{\text{RX}}}\,+\text{Mg}\underset{\text{ }}{\mathop{\xrightarrow{Dry\,\,\,ehter}}}\,\underset{Alkyl\,\,magnesium\,\,halide}{\mathop{\text{RMgX}}}\,\text{ }$

The carbon-magnesium bond is covalent but highly polar, with carbon pulling away electrons from electropositive magnesium but the magnesium-halogen bond is essentially ionic,$\overset{\ominus }{\mathop{\text{R}}}\,\overset{\oplus }{\mathop{\text{M}}}\,\text{gX}$ The Grignard reagent belongs to a class of compounds called organometallic compounds, in which carbon is bonded to a metal like lithium, potassium, sodium, zinc, mercury etc. Each kind of organometallic compound has its own set of properties.

But whatever the metal, it is electro negative than carbon and the carbon metal bond is always highly polar. Although the organic group is not a full fledged carbanion but has considerable carbanionic character. The, organometallic compounds can serve as a source of carbon bearing negative charge.

So, when Grignard reagent is added to carbonyl compounds, the organic group attaches to carbon and magnesium to oxygen.

Products formed by Grignard synthesis:

(a) Formaldehyde yields primary alcohol

(b) Any other aldehyde yields secondary alcohol.

(c) Ketones yield tertiary alcohols.

NOTE: 

(1) Sterically hindered t-alcohols cannot be prepared by grignard reagent, but can be prepared using lithium alkyls. This is because lithium compounds are usually more reactive and yield a better product.

(2) For compounds like , the reaction is:

(3) Reaction of \[RMgX\]with epoxide ring

(4)

{1 alcohol will be formed because of the allylic nature of \[2\] carbon}

concepts.title: By Reduction of Aldehyde and Ketone by using LiAlH4
concepts.conceptBody:
By reduction 

The chemical reducing agents like Lithium aluminium hydride $\left( \text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right),{{\text{H}}_{2}}/\text{Ni},{{\text{B}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{6}}/\text{THF}$ etc. are used to produce alcohols.

$\mathbf{LiAl}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$

(a) $\text{R}-\text{CHO}\xrightarrow{LiAI{{H}_{4}}}-\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}$

(b)

(c) $\text{R}-\text{COOH}\xrightarrow{LiAI{{H}_{4}}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}$

(d) ${{\text{R}}^{\prime }}-\text{COOR}\xrightarrow{LiAI{{H}_{4}}}{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{OH}-+\text{ROH}$

(e) $\text{R}-\text{COCl}\xrightarrow{LiAI{{H}_{4}}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{OH}$

(f) ${{(\text{RCO})}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{LiAI{{H}_{4}}}\text{RC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{OH}$

Note: $\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ does not reduce double or triple bonds however if double or triple bonds is in conjugation with $(\text{C}=\text{O})$ group, then double or triple bond is also reduced.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}=\text{CH}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{CHO}\xrightarrow{LiAI{{H}_{4}}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}=\text{CH}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}$

$\text{Ph}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CHO}\xrightarrow{\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}}\text{Ph}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}$

concepts.title: By Reduction of Aldehyde and Ketone by using NaBH4
concepts.conceptBody:
Using$\mathbf{NaB}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$ 

It can reduce all the groups which are reduced by $\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ but cannot reduce carboxylic acid and ester. It can also reduce only the double bond which are in conjugation with $\text{C}=\text{O}$ group.

concepts.title: By Reduction of Aldehyde and Ketone by using H2/Ni
concepts.conceptBody:
By ${{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{/Ni}$ 

It can reduce all the groups reduced by $\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ and it can also reduce double and triple bonds in the carbonyl compounds irrespective of the position.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CHO}\xrightarrow{{{\text{H}}_{2}}/\text{N}i}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{OH}$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}=\text{CH}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{CHO}\xrightarrow{{{\text{H}}_{2}}/\text{N}i}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{OH}$

Note: Hydrogenolysis (cleavage by hydrogen) of an ester requires more severe conditions than simple hydrogenation of (addition of hydrogen to) a carbon-carbon double bond. High pressure and elevated temperatures are required: the catalyst used most often is a mixture of oxides known as copper chromite, of approximately the composition CuO.$\text{CuC}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$. For example

$\underset{\text{ (Methyl dodecanoate) }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)}_{10}}\text{COOC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,\xrightarrow[150{}^\circ ,5000Ib/i{{n}^{2}}]{{{H}_{2}},CuO\,CuC{{r}_{_{2}{{O}_{4}}}}}C{{H}_{3}}{{\left( C{{H}_{2}} \right)}_{_{\left( 1-Dodecanol \right)}^{10}}}C{{H}_{3}}OH$

Chemical reduction is carried out by use of sodium metal and alcohol, or more usually by use of lithium aluminium hydride. For example

$\underset{\text{ (Ethyl hexadecanoate) }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)}_{14}}\text{COO}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}}}\,\xrightarrow{LiAI{{H}_{4}}}\underset{\text{ 1-Hexadecanol }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\left( C{{H}_{2}} \right)}_{14}}}}\,\underset{{}}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}OH}}\,$

We have learnt that one molecule of an ester on reaction with excess of Grignard reagent followed by acidification produces a tertiary alcohol.

This simple reaction has a beautiful application in synthesis of cyclic tertiary alcohols, from which alicyclic hydrocarbons can be synthesized.

concepts.title: By Reduction of Aldehyde and Ketone by using B2H6
concepts.conceptBody:
By ${{\mathbf{B}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{6}}}\mathbf{/THF}$ 

This reduces all the groups reduced by LiAlH4 except acid chloride ($-\text{COCl})$. It does reduce the double / Triple bond but Selectively.

concepts.title: Physical Properties of Thiols
concepts.conceptBody:
Thiols

Nomenclature: 

Thiols are the sulfur analogues of alcohols.

Functional group suffix $=-$ thiol

Functional group prefix = mercapto

Physical Properties:

  • Hydrogen bonding is much weaker than that in alcohols.
  • Lower boiling points than similar alcohols.

Structure:

  • Generally similar to alcohols, but bonds to$\text{S}$ are longer and weaker than those to $\text{O}$.
  • The thiol functional group consists of an $\text{S}$ atom bonded to a $\text{C}$ atom and a $\text{H}$ atom via $\sigma $ bonds.
  • The $\text{S}-\text{H}$ bonds is less polar than that in alcohols since $\text{S}$ is less electronegativity than $\text{O}$.

Reactivity:

  • Thiols are much more acidic than similar alcohols, e.g., $\text{RSH}(\text{pKa}=10)$ versus ROH $(\text{pKa}$ $=16$ to 19)
